title: Mount Kosciuszko

infobox.value: * [Country high point](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_highest_point "List of countries by highest point") * [Ultra](/wiki/Ultra-prominent_peak "Ultra-prominent peak")
[Main Range](/wiki/Main_Range_(Snowy_Mountains) "Main Range (Snowy Mountains)"), [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range")

([/ˌkɒziˈʌskoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KOZ-ee-USK-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[4]](#cite_note-4) Polish pronunciation: [[kɔɕˈt͡ɕuʂ.kɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [*kosh-CHOOSH-koh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Ngarigo](/wiki/Ngarigo_language "Ngarigo language"): *Kunama Namadgi*)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-dual_name_ABC_2019-6) is the highest mountain of mainland [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"), at 2,228 metres (7,310 ft) above sea level. It is located on the [Main Range](/wiki/Main_Range_(Snowy_Mountains) "Main Range (Snowy Mountains)") of the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") in [Kosciuszko National Park](/wiki/Kosciuszko_National_Park "Kosciuszko National Park"), a part of the [Australian Alps National Parks and Reserves](/wiki/Australian_Alps_National_Parks_and_Reserves "Australian Alps National Parks and Reserves"), in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), and is located west of Crackenback and close to [Jindabyne](/wiki/Jindabyne "Jindabyne"), near the border with [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia"). Mount Kosciuszko is ranked 35th by [topographic isolation](/wiki/Topographic_isolation "Topographic isolation").

**Mount Kosciuszko** ([/ˌkɒziˈʌskoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KOZ-ee-USK-oh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[4]](#cite_note-4) Polish pronunciation: [[kɔɕˈt͡ɕuʂ.kɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [*kosh-CHOOSH-koh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Ngarigo](/wiki/Ngarigo_language "Ngarigo language"): *Kunama Namadgi*)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-dual_name_ABC_2019-6) is the highest mountain of mainland [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"), at 2,228 metres (7,310 ft) above sea level. It is located on the [Main Range](/wiki/Main_Range_(Snowy_Mountains) "Main Range (Snowy Mountains)") of the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") in [Kosciuszko National Park](/wiki/Kosciuszko_National_Park "Kosciuszko National Park"), a part of the [Australian Alps National Parks and Reserves](/wiki/Australian_Alps_National_Parks_and_Reserves "Australian Alps National Parks and Reserves"), in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), and is located west of Crackenback and close to [Jindabyne](/wiki/Jindabyne "Jindabyne"), near the border with [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia"). Mount Kosciuszko is ranked 35th by [topographic isolation](/wiki/Topographic_isolation "Topographic isolation").

## Etymology and charting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and charting")]

The mountain was named by the [Polish](/wiki/Poland "Poland") explorer [Paweł (Paul) Edmund Strzelecki](/wiki/Pawe%C5%82_Strzelecki "Paweł Strzelecki") in 1840, in honour of [Tadeusz Kościuszko](/wiki/Tadeusz_Ko%C5%9Bciuszko "Tadeusz Kościuszko"), general and hero of the [Kościuszko Uprising](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bciuszko_Uprising "Kościuszko Uprising") and [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), because of its perceived resemblance to the [Kościuszko Mound](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bciuszko_Mound "Kościuszko Mound") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), Poland.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

An exploration party led by Strzelecki and [James Macarthur](/wiki/James_Macarthur_(politician) "James Macarthur (politician)") beside him with Indigenous guides [Charley Tarra](/wiki/Charley_Tarra "Charley Tarra") and Jackey set off on what is called Strzelecki’s Southern expedition. Macarthur was seeking new pastures. Strzelecki wanted to investigate the [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate"), [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [paleontology](/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology") and geography of NSW and to publish his findings.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This included identifying Australia’s highest summit, which Strzelecki reached on 12 March 1840.[[9]](#cite_note-Andrews-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Eug%C3%A8ne_von_Gu%C3%A9rard_-_Mount_Kosciusko%2C_seen_from_the_Victorian_border_%28Mount_Hope_Ranges%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Eug%C3%A8ne_von_Gu%C3%A9rard_-_Mount_Kosciusko%2C_seen_from_the_Victorian_border_%28Mount_Hope_Ranges%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eug%C3%A8ne_von_Gu%C3%A9rard_-_Mount_Kosciusko,_seen_from_the_Victorian_border_(Mount_Hope_Ranges)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Mount Kosciusko, seen from the Victorian border (Mount Hope Ranges)—*the mountain range as depicted by [Eugene von Guerard](/wiki/Eugene_von_Guerard "Eugene von Guerard"), 1866

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Guerard_Mount_Townsend_1863.jpg/250px-Guerard_Mount_Townsend_1863.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guerard_Mount_Townsend_1863.jpg)

*North-east view from the northern top of Mount Kosciusko—*[Eugene von Guerard](/wiki/Eugene_von_Guerard "Eugene von Guerard"), 1863

The approach was made from Geehi Valley. After climbing Hannel’s Spur, the peak now named [Mount Townsend](/wiki/Mount_Townsend_(Snowy_Mountains) "Mount Townsend (Snowy Mountains)") was reached. Here Strzelecki used his instruments to make observations. Mt Townsend is Australia's second highest mountain, adjacent to and almost the same height as Mt Kosciuszko, and Strzelecki saw that the neighbouring peak was slightly higher. In the presence of Macarthur he named the higher summit Mount Kosciusko after the famous Polish-Lithuanian military leader who died in 1817. As it was late, Macarthur decided to return to camp and Strzelecki alone climbed the Kosciuszko summit.

Based on Strzelecki’s records, Australia’s highest summit was mapped. A cartographical mistake made in an edition of Victorian maps transposed Mount Kosciusko to the position of the present Mount Townsend. Later editions of the map continued to show the original location.[[11]](#cite_note-11) NSW maps did not make this mistake.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Victorian error created confusion. In 1885, Austrian explorer [Robert von Lendenfeld](/wiki/Robert_J._Lendlmayer_von_Lendenfeld "Robert J. Lendlmayer von Lendenfeld"), guided by James M. Spencer,[[12]](#cite_note-Spencer-12) a local pastoralist, aided by a map containing the transposition error, reached Mount Townsend believing it was Mount Kosciusko. According to Spencer, the local Aboriginals called Mount Kosciusko *Tar-gan-gil*. Like Strzelecki, Lendenfeld also observed that the neighbouring peak was higher. He named it Mount Townsend to honour the surveyor who in 1846 traversed the peak.

Lendenfeld claimed he had identified and reached the highest peak of the continent. The NSW Department of Mines discovered Lendenfeld's mistake and assigned the name Mount Townsend to the second-highest mountain of the range. Lendenfeld's announcement created further confusion. When Lendenfeld's mistake was corrected, a popular legend was created that the established names of the two mountains were swapped rather than re-educate the populace of the name of the highest mountain.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The confusion was straightened out in 1940 by B. T. Dowd,[[14]](#cite_note-14) a cartographer and historian of the NSW Lands Department. His study reaffirmed that the mountain named by Strzelecki as Mount Kosciuszko was indeed, as the NSW maps had always shown, Australia’s highest summit. When Macarthur’s field book of the historical journey was published in 1941 by C. Daley,[[15]](#cite_note-15) it further confirmed Dowd’s clarification. This means that Targangil, mentioned in Spencer’s 1885 article,[[12]](#cite_note-Spencer-12) was the indigenous name of Mount Townsend, not of Mount Kosciusko. According to A. E. J. Andrews, Mount Kosciuszko had no indigenous name.[[16]](#cite_note-Andrews2-16) Detailed analysis of the mountain history can be found in books by H. P. G. Clews[[17]](#cite_note-17) and in the cited A.E.J. Andrews' book *Kosciusko: The Mountain in History*.[[9]](#cite_note-Andrews-9)

The name of the mountain was previously spelled "Mount Kosciusko", an Anglicisation, but the spelling "Mount Kosciuszko" was officially adopted in 1997 by the [Geographical Names Board of New South Wales](/wiki/Geographical_Names_Board_of_New_South_Wales "Geographical Names Board of New South Wales").[[3]](#cite_note-gnbmount-3) The traditional English pronunciation of Kosciuszko is [/ˌkɒziˈʌskoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KOZ-ee-US-koh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), but the pronunciation [/kɒˈʃʊʃkoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*kosh-UUSH-koh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") is now sometimes used,[[18]](#cite_note-18) which is substantially closer to the Polish pronunciation [[kɔɕˈt͡ɕuʂkɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-Tadeusz_Ko%C5%9Bciuszko.ogg "File:Pl-Tadeusz Kościuszko.ogg") [*kosh-CHYUUSH-koh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key").

### Aboriginal names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Aboriginal names")]

There are several native Aboriginal ([Ngarigo](/wiki/Ngarigo_language "Ngarigo language")) names associated with Mount Townsend, where J. Macarthur recorded in 1840 some campings of the natives. There is some confusion as to the exact sounds. These are *Jagungal, Jar-gan-gil, Tar-gan-gil, Tackingal*; however, all of them mean [bogong moth](/wiki/Bogong_moth "Bogong moth"), which [aestivate](/wiki/Aestivate "Aestivate") on the mountain.[[16]](#cite_note-Andrews2-16)

In 2019, "Kunama Namadgi" was submitted to the [Geographical Names Board of New South Wales](/wiki/Geographical_Names_Board_of_New_South_Wales "Geographical Names Board of New South Wales") as a proposed [dual name](/wiki/Dual_naming "Dual naming") for Mount Kosciuszko. The proposal was submitted by the Toomaroombah Kunama Namadgi Indigenous Corporation, which states that the proposed name means "snow" and "mountain". According to Uncle John Casey, the mountain's [Ngarigo](/wiki/Ngarigo "Ngarigo") name has "been Kunama Namadgi for 4,000 years, since we've been on country, until the white man came in the early 1800s and that's when they changed it". However, Iris White, the chairperson of the Southern Kosciuszko Executive Advisory Committee, disputed that account, stating "that name is not from our language. It's offensive because in some of our languages 'Kunama' actually means faeces". White said that a new name should not be given "just for the sake of it sounding Aboriginal or sounding good".[[6]](#cite_note-dual_name_ABC_2019-6)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

The mountain was formed by [geologic uplift](/wiki/Orogeny "Orogeny").[[19]](#cite_note-geologypage.com-19) It was not formed by any recent volcanic activity.[[20]](#cite_note-vtd-20) Eroded granite intrusions remain at the summit as large boulders above the more heavily eroded sedimentary rocks.[[20]](#cite_note-vtd-20)

Plant species found in the mountain include:

* Kosciuszko buttercup (*[Ranunculus anemoneus](/wiki/Ranunculus_anemoneus "Ranunculus anemoneus")*)
* Vickery's grass (*[Rytidosperma](/wiki/Rytidosperma "Rytidosperma") vickeryae*)
* [Phebalium](/wiki/Phebalium "Phebalium") (*Nematalolepis ovatifolia*)
* Billy buttons ([*Craspedia*](/wiki/Craspedia_(plant) "Craspedia (plant)") spp.)
* [Snow gum](/wiki/Snow_gum "Snow gum")

## Reaching the summit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reaching the summit")]

Mount Kosciuszko is the [highest](/wiki/Extremes_on_Earth "Extremes on Earth") summit in mainland Australia. Until 1977 it was possible to drive from [Charlotte Pass](/wiki/Charlotte_Pass,_New_South_Wales "Charlotte Pass, New South Wales") to within a few metres of the summit, but in 1977 the road was closed to public motor vehicle access due to environmental concerns. The road is open from Charlotte Pass for walkers and cyclists for 7.6 kilometres (5 mi)[[21]](#cite_note-21) to Rawson Pass, at an elevation of 2,100 metres (6,900 ft) above sea level. From there a 1.4-kilometre (1 mi) walking path leads to the summit.

The peak may also be approached from [Thredbo](/wiki/Thredbo,_New_South_Wales "Thredbo, New South Wales"), taking 3 to 3.5 hours for a round trip. This straightforward walk starts from the top of the Thredbo Kosciuszko Express [chairlift](/wiki/Chairlift "Chairlift"), which operates all year-round. The walking path is popular in summer, and is a mesh walkway to protect the native vegetation and prevent erosion. It is 5 kilometres (3.1 mi) to Rawson Pass, where it meets the track from Charlotte Pass, and from where it is a further 1.4 kilometres (0.87 mi) to the summit.
The walk to the summit is the easiest of all the [Seven Summits](/wiki/Seven_Summits "Seven Summits").[[22]](#cite_note-ctss-22)

Australia's highest [public toilet](/wiki/Public_toilet "Public toilet") was built at Rawson pass in 2007,[[23]](#cite_note-23) to cope with the more than 100,000 people visiting the mountain each summer.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The third and often overlooked route up Mount Kosciuszko is up the very challenging and historic **Hannel's Spur Track** (15.5 km or 9.6 mi), which approaches from the NW and is the only route to pass through the Western Fall Wilderness Zone – passing through four different bio-diversity bands along the ascent. The Hannel's Spur Track is officially Australia's biggest vertical ascent of 1,800 m (5,900 ft). This is the same route that explorer Paul Strzelecki climbed and discovered Kosciuszko in 1840 and also the same annual route that the stockmen once brought the cattle up/down from the valley almost 2 km (1.2 mi) below to graze in the alpine meadows of Kosi throughout the summer. The various aboriginal tribes from the Murray valley also used this same route annually for millennia to access Kosciuszko to harvest the delicacies of bogong moths that were abundant throughout the summer months and to socialise with other tribes from the coast and northern plains. The Hannel's Spur Track trailhead (sign) is about a 1.4-kilometre (0.87 mi) hike SSE of the Geehi Rest Area on the Alpine Way road between the towns of Thredbo and Khancoban.

The peak and the surrounding areas are snow-covered in winter and spring (usually beginning in June and continuing until October or later). The road from Charlotte Pass is marked by snow poles and provides a guide for [cross-country skiers](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing "Cross-country skiing"), and the track from Thredbo is easily followed until covered by snow in winter.

### Recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Mt_Kosciuszko_map_Stevage.png/330px-Mt_Kosciuszko_map_Stevage.png)](/wiki/File:Mt_Kosciuszko_map_Stevage.png)

Topographic map of Mount Kosciuszko including the approaches from Charlotte Pass and Thredbo.

Kosciuszko National Park is also the location of the downhill ski slopes closest to [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), containing the [Thredbo](/wiki/Thredbo,_New_South_Wales "Thredbo, New South Wales"), [Charlotte Pass](/wiki/Charlotte_Pass,_New_South_Wales "Charlotte Pass, New South Wales"), and [Perisher](/wiki/Perisher_Ski_Resort "Perisher Ski Resort") ski resorts. Mount Kosciuszko may have been ascended by [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") long before the first recorded ascent by Europeans.

Each year in December, an [ultramarathon](/wiki/Ultramarathon "Ultramarathon") running race called the [Coast to Kosciuszko](/wiki/Coast_to_Kosciuszko "Coast to Kosciuszko") ascends to the top of Mount Kosciuszko after starting at the coast 240 kilometres (150 mi) away.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Higher Australian mountains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Higher Australian mountains")]

Higher peaks exist within territory administered or claimed by Australia: outside the continent are [Mawson Peak](/wiki/Mawson_Peak "Mawson Peak") (2,745 m or 9,006 ft) on [Heard Island](/wiki/Heard_Island_and_McDonald_Islands "Heard Island and McDonald Islands") as well as [Dome Argus](/wiki/Dome_A "Dome A") (4,030 m or 13,220 ft), [Mount McClintock](/wiki/Mount_McClintock "Mount McClintock") (3,490 m or 11,450 ft) and [Mount Menzies](/wiki/Mount_Menzies "Mount Menzies") (3,355 m or 11,007 ft) in the [Australian Antarctic Territory](/wiki/Australian_Antarctic_Territory "Australian Antarctic Territory").

Although not in Australia, [Puncak Jaya](/wiki/Puncak_Jaya "Puncak Jaya") in [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), which stands at 4,884 m or 16,024 ft, is the highest mountain in the [Australian continent](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") as well as [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Kosciuszko&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The 1863 picture by [Eugene von Guerard](/wiki/Eugene_von_Guerard "Eugene von Guerard") hanging in the [National Gallery of Australia](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Australia "National Gallery of Australia") titled *Northeast view from the northern top of Mount Kosciusko* is actually from [Mount Townsend](/wiki/Mount_Townsend_(Snowy_Mountains) "Mount Townsend (Snowy Mountains)").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In the well known poem by [Banjo Paterson](/wiki/Banjo_Paterson "Banjo Paterson"), [The Man From Snowy River](/wiki/The_Man_from_Snowy_River_(poem) "The Man from Snowy River (poem)") is said to hail from “up by Kosciusko’s side”.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Australian rock band [Midnight Oil](/wiki/Midnight_Oil "Midnight Oil") recorded a song called "Kosciusko" on its 1984 album *[Red Sails in the Sunset](/wiki/Red_Sails_in_the_Sunset_(album) "Red Sails in the Sunset (album)")*, referring to the mountain. The spelling was updated to "Kosciuszko" for the group's 1997 [compilation album](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album"), *[20,000 Watt R.S.L.](/wiki/20,000_Watt_R.S.L. "20,000 Watt R.S.L.")*

A species of lizard, *[Eulamprus kosciuskoi](/wiki/Eulamprus "Eulamprus")*, is named after Mount Kosciuszko.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The mountain top was the finish line on the [fifth season](/wiki/The_Amazing_Race_Australia_5 "The Amazing Race Australia 5") of *[The Amazing Race Australia](/wiki/The_Amazing_Race_Australia "The Amazing Race Australia")*.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
